content.value: A binary heap is a complete binary tree (filled left to right at every level) satisfying the heap property: in a max-heap, every parent is greater than or equal to its children; in a min-heap, every parent is less than or equal to its children. Heaps are usually stored in an array.
Heapify fixes a single violation of the heap property by comparing a node to its children and swapping with the larger (max-heap) child, repeating down the tree until the property holds.
Build a max-heap from the array, then repeatedly swap the root (the maximum) with the last element and shrink the heap, re-heapifying each time. Runs in O(n log n) and sorts in place.

A greedy algorithm that builds an optimal prefix-free binary code for data compression, using a min-heap. It repeatedly extracts the two lowest-frequency nodes, merges them into a new node with combined frequency, and reinserts that merged node, until only one node (the tree root) remains.

items: 1. Put every symbol into a min-heap, keyed by frequency
3. Create a new internal node with these two as children, frequency = sum of both
4. Insert the new node back into the heap
5. Repeat until one node remains - that's the Huffman tree root
6. Code for each symbol = path from root to leaf (0 for left, 1 for right)

content.value: Symbols that appear more frequently end up closer to the root in a Huffman tree, giving them shorter codes - this is exactly why Huffman coding compresses data.

quiz.question: In a max-heap stored as an array, where is the left child of index i?
Huffman's algorithm repeatedly merges which two nodes?
